tasks.title: Bug: Architect agent intermittently fails to write plan.json
tasks.description: The architect agent claims "plan written to .task/plan.json" in its output text but the file doesn't actually exist. Observed ~50% failure rate across orchestrator runs against the reify project. The orchestrator correctly detects this (artifacts.read_plan() returns empty) and blocks the task, but the wasted cost is significant ($1-2 per failed attempt). Workaround: reset task to pending and re-run. Root cause likely in how the agent uses the Write tool vs describing the plan in text output.
tasks.details: Observed during reify M1 orchestrator runs (2026-03-16). Task 1 failed in run 1, succeeded in run 2. Task 3 failed in run 3. The architect system prompt includes Write in --allowed-tools and instructs "Write the plan to .task/plan.json using the Write tool." The agent returns success with turns=15-32 and describes the plan in its result text, but the file is absent on disk. Possible causes: (1) agent describes the plan instead of calling Write, (2) Write tool call fails silently, (3) cwd mismatch between agent and worktree.

tasks.title: Bug: Verify step fails with `source: not found` on dash-based systems
tasks.description: verify.py uses asyncio.create_subprocess_shell() which invokes /bin/sh. On systems where /bin/sh is dash (e.g. Ubuntu), the `source` builtin doesn't exist — only `.` works. Verification commands like `source ~/.cargo/env && cargo test` fail with `/bin/sh: 1: source: not found`, causing tasks to exhaust verify attempts and block even when the code is correct.
tasks.details: Fix options: (1) Use `. ` instead of `source` in config commands (user-side workaround, already applied to reify config). (2) Pass executable='/bin/bash' to create_subprocess_shell in verify.py. (3) Document that verify commands must be POSIX-compatible. Option 2 is the proper fix since it doesn't burden config authors with shell compatibility knowledge.

tasks.title: Bug: Reviewers receive empty diff, rubber-stamp PASS
tasks.description: During the review stage, `git diff main...HEAD` sometimes returns an empty diff. All 5 reviewers then report "PASS — empty diff, no issues" which defeats the purpose of review. Observed on task 4 (reify-geometry) during reify M1 run.
tasks.details: Likely cause: the merge-base calculation `main...HEAD` returns nothing when the worktree branch has already been fast-forwarded or when main has moved. The review happens after verification passes, but if the worktree was rebased or the diff reference is stale, the diff is empty. Fix: compute the diff at worktree creation time (store the base commit in .task/metadata.json) and use that as the diff base, e.g. `git diff <base-sha>...HEAD`.

tasks.title: Bug: git add -A in worktrees commits .task/ artifacts
tasks.description: The orchestrator's git_ops.commit() runs `git add -A` which stages .task/plan.json, .task/iterations.jsonl, and .task/reviews/*.json. These internal orchestrator artifacts get committed to the task branch and merged to main. Workaround: add `.task/` to project .gitignore. Proper fix: exclude .task/ in git_ops.commit() or use a more targeted staging approach.
tasks.details: In git_ops.py line 61: `await _run(['git', 'add', '-A'], cwd=worktree)`. Fix options: (1) Add .task/ to a .gitignore that's created in the worktree during TaskArtifacts.init(). (2) Change git add -A to explicit file staging. (3) Run `git reset HEAD .task/` after `git add -A`. Option 1 is simplest — have artifacts.init() write a .task/.gitignore containing `*`.

tasks.title: Bug: Fused-memory task tools missing from Claude Code due to config path
tasks.description: When Claude Code connects to fused-memory via stdio, the default config (fused-memory/config/config.yaml) resolves the Taskmaster path as ./taskmaster-ai relative to the fused-memory directory, but taskmaster-ai lives at dark-factory/taskmaster-ai. Taskmaster init silently fails, causing all 11 task proxy tools to not be registered. The server logs a warning but the client has no way to know task tools are absent vs nonexistent.
tasks.details: The fix has two parts: (1) Project .mcp.json files should set CONFIG_PATH to a project-specific config with absolute Taskmaster paths, or set TASKMASTER_DIR env var. (2) The server should surface Taskmaster connection status to clients — e.g. include `taskmaster_connected: false` in get_status output, or mention it in the server instructions/capabilities so clients can detect the gap. Currently the failure is completely silent from the client's perspective.

tasks.title: Project scaffold, config, and app skeleton
tasks.description: Create the dashboard project structure under dashboard/ with all foundational files needed by subsequent tasks.
tasks.details:
Create the following files:

1. `dashboard/pyproject.toml` — hatchling build, dependencies: fastapi>=0.115, uvicorn[standard]>=0.34, jinja2>=3.1, httpx>=0.28, aiosqlite>=0.21. Dev extras: pytest>=8, pytest-asyncio>=0.25, httpx.

2. `dashboard/src/dashboard/__init__.py` — empty.

3. `dashboard/src/dashboard/config.py` — DashboardConfig dataclass with fields: host="127.0.0.1", port=8080, project_root=Path("/home/leo/src/dark-factory"), fused_memory_url="http://localhost:8000", fused_memory_project_id="dark_factory". Properties for derived paths: reconciliation_db (project_root/"fused-memory"/"data"/"reconciliation"/"reconciliation.db"), write_queue_db (project_root/"fused-memory"/"data"/"queue"/"write_queue.db"), tasks_json (project_root/".taskmaster"/"tasks"/"tasks.json"), worktrees_dir (project_root/".worktrees"). Support env var overrides prefixed DASHBOARD_ (e.g. DASHBOARD_PORT).

4. `dashboard/src/dashboard/app.py` — FastAPI app with: Jinja2Templates pointing to templates dir, StaticFiles mount at /static, lifespan context manager (creates httpx.AsyncClient stored in app.state), GET /api/health returning {"status": "ok"}. Do NOT add other routes yet — those come in tasks 5-8.

5. `dashboard/src/dashboard/data/__init__.py` — empty.

6. `dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/base.html` — Jinja2 base template. Dark theme (bg-gray-900, text-gray-100). Head includes: Tailwind CSS CDN (script tag), htmx CDN (script tag), Alpine.js CDN (script tag), link to /static/style.css. Body has a container div with {% block content %}{% endblock %}. Header with "Dark Factory" title.

7. `dashboard/src/dashboard/static/style.css` — minimal custom styles if needed beyond Tailwind utilities.

8. `dashboard/tests/__init__.py` — empty.

9. `dashboard/tests/conftest.py` — shared pytest fixtures: a fixture that creates a DashboardConfig with temp paths, a fixture for FastAPI TestClient.

Test strategy: Run `cd dashboard && uv sync && uv run pytest tests/ -x` — verify project installs cleanly and conftest fixtures work. Write a minimal test in conftest or a test_scaffold.py that imports dashboard.config and dashboard.app and asserts the health endpoint returns 200.

tasks.title: Memory data module
tasks.description: Create data/memory.py — the data access layer for memory health metrics from the fused-memory MCP HTTP server.
tasks.details:
Create `dashboard/src/dashboard/data/memory.py` with:

1. `async def mcp_tool_call(client: httpx.AsyncClient, base_url: str, tool_name: str, arguments: dict) -> dict` — POST JSON-RPC to `{base_url}/mcp` with body `{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "method": "tools/call", "params": {"name": tool_name, "arguments": arguments}}`. Parse response: extract `result.content[0].text`, json.loads it, return dict. Timeout: 10s.

2. `async def get_memory_status(client: httpx.AsyncClient, config: DashboardConfig) -> dict` — call MCP tool `get_status` with `{"project_id": config.fused_memory_project_id}`. Returns dict with keys: graphiti (connected, node_count), mem0 (connected, memory_count), queue (counts dict, oldest_pending_age_seconds), taskmaster (connected). On httpx.ConnectError or httpx.TimeoutException, return `{"offline": True, "error": str(e)}`.

3. `async def get_queue_stats(client: httpx.AsyncClient, config: DashboardConfig) -> dict` — call MCP tool `get_queue_stats` with `{}`. Returns dict with counts (pending, in_flight, retry, completed, dead) and oldest_pending_age_seconds. Same error handling as above.

Create `dashboard/tests/test_memory.py` with tests:
- Test successful get_memory_status: mock httpx to return a valid MCP response, verify parsed output matches expected structure.
- Test successful get_queue_stats: same pattern.
- Test offline fallback: mock httpx to raise ConnectError, verify returns {"offline": True, ...}.
- Test malformed response: mock httpx to return unexpected JSON, verify graceful handling (returns empty dict or raises clear error).

Use httpx's MockTransport or pytest-httpx for mocking.

tasks.title: Reconciliation data module
tasks.description: Create data/reconciliation.py — async SQLite queries for reconciliation metrics from reconciliation.db (read-only).
tasks.details:
Create `dashboard/src/dashboard/data/reconciliation.py` with:

All functions open the database read-only: `aiosqlite.connect(f"file:{db_path}?mode=ro", uri=True)`.
All functions accept a `db_path: Path` parameter and handle FileNotFoundError by returning empty defaults.

1. `async def get_recent_runs(db_path: Path, limit: int = 5) -> list[dict]` — query: `SELECT id, run_type, trigger_reason, started_at, completed_at, events_processed, status FROM runs ORDER BY started_at DESC LIMIT ?`. Return list of dicts. Calculate duration_seconds from completed_at - started_at for each run (both are ISO 8601 strings).

2. `async def get_watermarks(db_path: Path, project_id: str = "dark_factory") -> dict` — query: `SELECT * FROM watermarks WHERE project_id = ?`. Return dict with last_full_run_completed, last_episode_timestamp, last_memory_timestamp, last_task_change_timestamp. Return empty dict if no row.

3. `async def get_buffer_stats(db_path: Path) -> dict` — queries: `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM event_buffer WHERE status = 'buffered'` and `SELECT MIN(timestamp) FROM event_buffer WHERE status = 'buffered'`. Return `{"buffered_count": int, "oldest_event_age_seconds": float|None}`. Calculate age from oldest timestamp vs now.

4. `async def get_burst_state(db_path: Path) -> list[dict]` — query: `SELECT agent_id, state, last_write_at, burst_started_at FROM burst_state`. Return list of dicts.

5. `async def get_latest_verdict(db_path: Path) -> dict | None` — query: `SELECT run_id, severity, action_taken, reviewed_at FROM judge_verdicts ORDER BY reviewed_at DESC LIMIT 1`. Return dict or None.

Create `dashboard/tests/test_reconciliation.py`:
- Fixture: create an in-memory SQLite db, execute CREATE TABLE statements matching the reconciliation.db schema (watermarks, runs, event_buffer, burst_state, judge_verdicts tables — see PRD for exact schemas), insert sample rows.
- Test each query function against the fixture DB.
- Test missing database file returns empty defaults.
- Test empty tables return empty lists/dicts/None.

SQLite schemas for the fixture (from the actual codebase):
- `CREATE TABLE watermarks (project_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, last_full_run_id TEXT, last_full_run_completed TEXT, last_episode_timestamp TEXT, last_memory_timestamp TEXT, last_task_change_timestamp TEXT)`
- `CREATE TABLE runs (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, project_id TEXT NOT NULL, run_type TEXT NOT NULL, trigger_reason TEXT NOT NULL, started_at TEXT NOT NULL, completed_at TEXT, events_processed INTEGER DEFAULT 0, stage_reports TEXT DEFAULT '{}', status TEXT DEFAULT 'running')`
- `CREATE TABLE event_buffer (id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, project_id TEXT NOT NULL, event_type TEXT NOT NULL, event_source TEXT NOT NULL, agent_id TEXT, timestamp TEXT NOT NULL, payload TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '{}', status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'buffered')`
- `CREATE TABLE burst_state (agent_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, state TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'idle', last_write_at TEXT NOT NULL, burst_started_at TEXT)`
- `CREATE TABLE judge_verdicts (run_id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, reviewed_at TEXT NOT NULL, severity TEXT NOT NULL, findings TEXT DEFAULT '[]', action_taken TEXT DEFAULT 'none')`

tasks.title: Orchestrator discovery module
tasks.description: Create data/orchestrator.py — process scanning, task tree parsing, and worktree artifact reading for orchestrator status.
tasks.details:
Create `dashboard/src/dashboard/data/orchestrator.py` with:

1. `def find_running_orchestrators() -> list[dict]` — run `subprocess.run(["ps", "aux"], capture_output=True, text=True)`. For each line containing "orchestrator" and "--prd", extract PID (field 1), start time (fields 8-9), and PRD path (regex `--prd\s+(\S+)`). Return list of `{"pid": int, "prd": str, "running": True, "started": str}`. Filter out the grep/ps process itself.

2. `def load_task_tree(tasks_json_path: Path) -> list[dict]` — read and parse `.taskmaster/tasks/tasks.json`. Extract `data["master"]["tasks"]` (or `data["tasks"]` — handle both formats). Return list of task dicts with keys: id, title, status, priority, dependencies, and metadata (if present, includes modules). Handle FileNotFoundError → return []. Handle malformed JSON → log warning, return [].

3. `def read_task_artifacts(worktree_path: Path) -> dict` — Read orchestrator artifacts from a worktree's `.task/` directory:
   - `.task/metadata.json`: parse for task_id, title, base_commit, created_at
   - `.task/plan.json`: parse steps array, count done vs total → derive phase (PLAN if no plan, EXECUTE/VERIFY/REVIEW/MERGE based on step progress, DONE if all done)
   - `.task/iterations.jsonl`: count lines (don't parse each line)
   - `.task/reviews/*.json`: glob, parse each, count PASS vs ISSUES_FOUND → return summary like "3/5 PASS"
   All wrapped in try/except, return None/0/"—" for missing files.
   Returns: `{"metadata": dict|None, "phase": str, "plan_progress": {"done": int, "total": int}, "iteration_count": int, "review_summary": str}`.

4. `def discover_orchestrators(config: DashboardConfig) -> list[dict]` — combine process scan + worktree scan:
   - Get running orchestrators from find_running_orchestrators()
   - Get task tree from load_task_tree(config.tasks_json)
   - Scan config.worktrees_dir for subdirectories, read artifacts from each
   - For each running orchestrator: attach the task tree and per-task artifacts
   - Compute summary stats: total, done, in_progress, blocked, pending counts
   - Return list of orchestrator dicts with all combined data

Create `dashboard/tests/test_orchestrator.py`:
- Test find_running_orchestrators: mock subprocess.run to return canned ps output with orchestrator lines. Verify PID and PRD extraction.
- Test load_task_tree: create a temp tasks.json with sample data. Verify parsing. Test missing file. Test malformed JSON.
- Test read_task_artifacts: create temp dir with .task/ subdirectory containing sample metadata.json, plan.json (with mix of done/pending steps), iterations.jsonl (3 lines), reviews/reviewer-1.json (PASS), reviews/reviewer-2.json (ISSUES_FOUND). Verify all fields parsed correctly.
- Test discover_orchestrators: mock process scan, create temp worktrees dir with one worktree containing artifacts. Verify combined output.

tasks.title: Memory health cards partial and route
tasks.description: Create the memory health cards HTML partial template and its FastAPI route, wiring data from the memory data module.
tasks.details:
Create `dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/partials/memory.html`:

Four cards in a horizontal flex row (flex, gap-4). Each card: bg-gray-800, rounded-lg, p-6, shadow.

1. **Graphiti card**: Status dot (green if connected, red if not — use a 3x3 rounded-full div with bg-green-500/bg-red-500). "Graphiti" label (text-sm text-gray-400). Node count as large number (text-3xl font-bold text-white). Subtitle "knowledge graph nodes" (text-xs text-gray-500).

2. **Mem0 card**: Same pattern. Memory count. Subtitle "vector memories".

3. **Taskmaster card**: Same dot pattern. "Taskmaster" label. Text "Connected" or "Disconnected" (instead of a number).

4. **Write Queue card**: Tri-state dot: green if all counts are 0, yellow if pending > 0, red if dead > 0. "Write Queue" label. Show three metrics inline: "N pending · N retry · N dead" with dead count in red text if > 0. If oldest_pending_age_seconds is not None and > 60, show "oldest: Xm ago" in yellow text.

If the data has `offline: True`, show a single full-width card: "Fused Memory Server Offline" with red styling and the error message.

Add to `dashboard/src/dashboard/app.py`:
- `GET /partials/memory` async route: get httpx client from request.app.state, call get_memory_status() and get_queue_stats() from dashboard.data.memory, render templates/partials/memory.html with the data as template context, return HTMLResponse.
- Import the data functions at the top of app.py.

Test strategy: verify the route returns 200 with text/html content-type. Test with mock data module returning both online and offline states. Check that the HTML contains expected elements (e.g., "Graphiti", "Mem0", node count value).

tasks.title: Reconciliation panel partial and route
tasks.description: Create the reconciliation panel HTML partial template and its FastAPI route, wiring data from the reconciliation data module.
tasks.details:
Create `dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/partials/recon.html`:

Two-column grid layout (grid grid-cols-2 gap-6). Dark themed cards (bg-gray-800 rounded-lg p-6).

**Left column — Current State:**

- **Event Buffer** section: Show "N events buffered". If oldest_event_age_seconds is not None, show "oldest: Xm Xs ago". Use yellow text if age > 300s.

- **Burst State** table: columns agent_id, state. State "bursting" in orange badge, "idle" in gray badge. Show "No agents" if empty.

- **Watermarks** section: Show each timestamp as relative time (implement a Jinja2 filter `timeago` that converts ISO 8601 to "Xm ago" / "Xh ago" / "Xd ago" relative to now). Fields: Last full run, Last episode, Last memory, Last task change. Use text-gray-400 for labels, text-white for values.

- **Latest Verdict**: Severity badge — bg-green-600 for "ok", bg-yellow-600 for "minor", bg-orange-600 for "moderate", bg-red-600 for "serious". Show action_taken next to it. Show "No verdicts yet" if None.

**Right column — Recent Runs (last 5):**

Table with columns: Trigger, Type, Status, Events, Duration, When.
- Status badges: bg-green-600=completed, bg-blue-600=running, bg-red-600=failed, bg-orange-600=rolled_back, bg-purple-600=circuit_breaker.
- Duration: show in seconds or "—" if still running.
- When: relative time (timeago filter).
- Show "No reconciliation runs yet" if empty.

Add to `dashboard/src/dashboard/app.py`:
- `GET /partials/recon` async route: call all 5 reconciliation query functions (get_recent_runs, get_watermarks, get_buffer_stats, get_burst_state, get_latest_verdict) with config.reconciliation_db and render the partial.
- Register the `timeago` Jinja2 filter on the templates environment.

The `timeago` filter: accept an ISO 8601 string, parse with datetime.fromisoformat, compute delta from now (UTC), return human string like "3m ago", "2h ago", "1d ago". Handle None → return "never".

Test strategy: verify route returns 200. Test with mock reconciliation data. Check HTML contains expected badges and table structure.

tasks.title: Orchestrator list and task detail partials and routes
tasks.description: Create the orchestrator list and task detail HTML partial templates and their FastAPI routes, wiring data from the orchestrator discovery module.
tasks.details:
Create `dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/partials/orchestrators.html`:

**Header**: "Orchestrators" (text-xl font-bold) + count of running instances as badge (bg-blue-600 rounded-full px-2 text-sm).

**Per-orchestrator card** (bg-gray-800 rounded-lg p-6 mb-4):
- **Header line**: PID with green "running" badge (bg-green-600 text-xs rounded px-2 py-0.5) or gray "completed" badge. PRD filename (extracted from path, text-gray-400).
- **Progress bar**: Full-width bar (h-3 rounded-full bg-gray-700 overflow-hidden). Colored segments inside as flex children: green (done), blue (in-progress), yellow (pending), red (blocked). Width proportional to count/total.
- **Stats row**: Flex with gap, each stat: count + label. "N done · N in-progress · N blocked · N pending" with matching colors.
- **Expand button**: "Show tasks" / "Hide tasks" toggle using Alpine.js `@click="open = !open"` and `x-show="open"`. Wrap card in `<div x-data="{ open: false }">`.

Create `dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/partials/tasks.html`:

**Task table** (inside the expandable section, rendered per orchestrator):
- Table with classes: w-full text-sm text-left text-gray-300.
- Header row: ID, Title, Status, Phase, Iterations, Reviews, Modules (bg-gray-700 text-gray-400 text-xs uppercase).
- Data rows: 
  - **Status** as badge: bg-green-600=done, bg-blue-600=in-progress, bg-yellow-600=pending, bg-red-600=blocked, bg-gray-600=cancelled/deferred.
  - **Phase**: short label (PLAN/EXEC/VERIFY/REVIEW/MERGE/DONE/—) with muted color.
  - **Iterations**: number or "—".
  - **Reviews**: e.g. "3/5 PASS" or "—".
  - **Modules**: comma-joined, text-xs text-gray-500, truncated with `truncate max-w-[200px]`.

If no orchestrators found, show a centered message: "No orchestrators detected" with gray text.

Add to `dashboard/src/dashboard/app.py`:
- `GET /partials/orchestrators` async route: call discover_orchestrators(config), render orchestrators.html with the orchestrator list. The tasks.html partial is included inline within orchestrators.html (use Jinja2 include or render tasks directly in the template loop).

Test strategy: verify route returns 200. Test with mock discover_orchestrators returning both empty list and a list with one running + one completed orchestrator. Check HTML contains progress bar, stats, task table elements.

tasks.title: Full page assembly, entry point, and integration tests
tasks.description: Create index.html combining all partials with htmx polling, add __main__.py entry point, and write integration tests for all routes.
tasks.details:
Create `dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/index.html`: Extends base.html. Content block contains 4 sections stacked vertically (space-y-8 max-w-7xl mx-auto p-6): 1. **Memory Health** section: `
Loading...
` 2. **Reconciliation** section: `
Loading...
` 3. **Orchestrators** section: `
Loading...
` 4. **Footer**: Small text "Dark Factory Dashboard" with current time (updated via Alpine.js `x-text` with a setInterval, or just static). Add to `dashboard/src/dashboard/app.py`: - `GET /` route: render index.html (no data needed — partials load themselves via htmx). Create `dashboard/src/dashboard/__main__.py`: ```python """Entry point: python -m dashboard.app""" import uvicorn from dashboard.config import DashboardConfig config = DashboardConfig() uvicorn.run("dashboard.app:app", host=config.host, port=config.port, reload=True) ``` Create `dashboard/tests/test_app.py` — integration tests using FastAPI TestClient: 1. **Test GET /** — returns 200, content-type text/html, body contains "Dark Factory", contains hx-get="/partials/memory", hx-get="/partials/recon", hx-get="/partials/orchestrators". 2. **Test GET /partials/memory** — mock dashboard.data.memory functions to return sample data. Returns 200, text/html, contains "Graphiti", "Mem0", "Taskmaster", "Write Queue". 3. **Test GET /partials/memory offline** — mock to return {"offline": True}. Returns 200, contains "Offline". 4. **Test GET /partials/recon** — mock dashboard.data.reconciliation functions. Returns 200, contains expected table elements. 5. **Test GET /partials/recon no data** — mock to return empty defaults. Returns 200, contains "No reconciliation runs". 6. **Test GET /partials/orchestrators** — mock dashboard.data.orchestrator.discover_orchestrators to return a sample orchestrator with tasks. Returns 200, contains progress bar elements, task table. 7. **Test GET /partials/orchestrators empty** — mock to return []. Returns 200, contains "No orchestrators detected". 8. **Test GET /api/health** — returns 200, application/json, body is {"status": "ok"}. Use `unittest.mock.patch` or `monkeypatch` to mock the data modules. Import the app and create TestClient in a fixture. Test strategy: `cd dashboard && uv run pytest tests/ -x -v`. All 8+ tests should pass. Also manually verify by running `cd dashboard && uv run python -m dashboard.app` and opening http://127.0.0.1:8080 in a browser.

tasks.title: Bug: Task reconciliation uses project_root as memory project_id
tasks.description: Task-triggered reconciliation currently emits events keyed by Taskmaster's `project_root` and then reuses that string as the memory `project_id`. That causes completion knowledge, memory hints, and related searches to land under a different Graphiti group / Mem0 collection than the rest of the project's memory, fragmenting the knowledge base.
tasks.details: The repo docs distinguish task routing (`project_root="/home/leo/src/dark-factory"`) from memory routing (`project_id="dark_factory"`), but the reconciliation path collapses them. Relevant code paths: `fused_memory.middleware.task_interceptor.TaskInterceptor._make_event`, `fused_memory.reconciliation.targeted.TargetedReconciler`, and `fused_memory.models.scope.Scope`. Fix should establish a canonical mapping from task project root to memory project_id and use it consistently across targeted and full reconciliation stages.
tasks.testStrategy: Add integration tests proving a task status transition writes memory into the canonical project scope (`dark_factory`) rather than the filesystem path. Cover both targeted reconciliation and any stage-base task tools that currently pass `project_root=project_id`.

tasks.title: Bug: add_episode returns an episode_id that is never written to Graphiti
tasks.description: MemoryService generates and returns an `episode_id` immediately from `add_episode()`, but that UUID is not included in the queued Graphiti write. The actual Graphiti episode therefore gets a different UUID, so the returned ID cannot be used reliably for provenance, follow-up reads, or deletion.
tasks.details: Relevant paths: `fused_memory.services.memory_service.MemoryService.add_episode`, `_execute_graphiti_write`, and `fused_memory.backends.graphiti_client.GraphitiBackend.add_episode`. The durable queue payload needs to carry the generated UUID through to Graphiti, or the API contract needs to change so the system does not promise a stable episode ID before the backend has accepted one.
tasks.testStrategy: Add a test that enqueues an episode, drains the queue, and asserts the returned `episode_id` matches the UUID passed into the Graphiti backend. Also cover delete/provenance flows that depend on the returned ID.

tasks.title: Bug: Unified Graphiti search/delete contract is internally inconsistent
tasks.description: The fused-memory API presents search results and deletion as a uniform cross-store contract, but Graphiti results currently return edge UUIDs with no category while `delete_memory(store="graphiti")` expects an episode UUID. Category filtering also silently drops all Graphiti results because their category is always null.
tasks.details: Relevant paths: `fused_memory.services.memory_service._search_graphiti`, `search(... categories=...)`, `delete_memory()`, and the MCP tool docs in `fused_memory.server.tools`. This needs either a real unified identifier contract or explicit store-specific semantics. At minimum the API/docs/tests should agree on what Graphiti IDs represent and whether category-filtered searches can include Graphiti facts.
tasks.testStrategy: Add coverage for: (1) Graphiti search results used as delete inputs, (2) category-filtered searches that include Graphiti results when appropriate, and (3) tool docs / response schema consistency around `memory_id`, `source_store`, and category handling.

tasks.title: Bug: EventBuffer quiescence check reads the wrong durable queue stats shape
tasks.description: Reconciliation's quiescence logic expects top-level `pending`, `retry`, and `in_flight` fields from the durable write queue, but `DurableWriteQueue.get_stats()` returns those counts nested under `counts`. That can make the system think the queue is idle and trigger reconciliation while Graphiti writes are still outstanding.
tasks.details: Relevant paths: `fused_memory.reconciliation.event_buffer.EventBuffer._is_quiescent` and `fused_memory.services.durable_queue.DurableWriteQueue.get_stats`. The current tests mock the flat shape instead of the real one, so the mismatch passes unnoticed. Fix the consumer, then align the tests with the actual queue contract.
tasks.testStrategy: Add a regression test that wires `EventBuffer` to a real or realistically mocked `DurableWriteQueue.get_stats()` response and proves quiescence stays false while `counts.pending + counts.retry + counts.in_flight > 0`.

tasks.title: Fix HTMX polling destroying Alpine.js state on orchestrators panel
tasks.description:
The orchestrators partial uses hx-swap="innerHTML" with every-10s polling. Each HTMX refresh re-initializes Alpine.js x-data="{ open: false }" components, causing any expanded "Show tasks" accordion to collapse every 10 seconds. This makes the only interactive element on the dashboard completely broken in practice.

The fix must preserve Alpine.js component state across HTMX partial refreshes. Options:
1. Use the HTMX idiomorph extension (hx-swap="morph:innerHTML") which diffs the DOM and preserves Alpine state.
2. Move the toggle state out of Alpine into a URL hash or localStorage, and restore it on HTMX swap.
3. Use hx-swap="outerHTML" with hx-select to swap only data elements, keeping the Alpine container intact.

Option 1 (idiomorph) is the simplest and most maintainable. It requires adding the idiomorph extension script tag to base.html and changing the hx-swap attribute on the orchestrators section in index.html.

The fix should also apply to the other polling sections (memory, recon) for consistency, even though they don't currently use Alpine.
tasks.details:
Files to modify:
- dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/base.html — add idiomorph extension script tag
- dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/index.html — change hx-swap="innerHTML" to hx-swap="morph:innerHTML" on all three polling sections

The idiomorph extension CDN: https://unpkg.com/idiomorph@0.3.0/dist/idiomorph-ext.min.js (pin the version). Add hx-ext="morph" to the body tag or to each section.

Test: After the fix, expanding "Show tasks" on an orchestrator card should survive HTMX poll cycles without collapsing. Verify by running the dashboard, clicking Show tasks, and waiting >10s.
tasks.testStrategy: Write a test that renders the orchestrators partial twice with different data and verifies the HTML structure is morph-compatible (stable element IDs/structure). Also verify the idiomorph script tag is present in base.html output.

tasks.title: Fix httpx not following redirects for fused-memory MCP calls
tasks.description:
The dashboard shows "Fused Memory Server Offline" with error "MCP call get_status returned non-200 status: 307" even when the fused-memory server is running. This is because httpx.AsyncClient() defaults to follow_redirects=False, and the MCP server returns a 307 redirect (likely trailing-slash normalization from /mcp/ to /mcp or vice versa).

Fix: Either set follow_redirects=True on the httpx.AsyncClient in app.py's lifespan, or remove the trailing slash from the URL in memory.py's mcp_tool_call (currently posts to {base_url}/mcp/).

Both fixes should be applied: follow_redirects=True as a safety net, and correct the URL to avoid the redirect entirely.
tasks.details:
Files to modify:
- dashboard/src/dashboard/app.py line 57 — change httpx.AsyncClient() to httpx.AsyncClient(follow_redirects=True)
- dashboard/src/dashboard/data/memory.py line 34 — verify the URL path matches what the fused-memory HTTP server expects (check if it's /mcp or /mcp/)

Test by curling the fused-memory server directly to determine the correct URL:
  curl -v -X POST http://localhost:8000/mcp/ vs http://localhost:8000/mcp
tasks.testStrategy: Add a test that verifies httpx.AsyncClient is created with follow_redirects=True. Add a test that mocks a 307 response and verifies the client follows it rather than returning offline status.

tasks.title: Make recon panel responsive and truncate raw trigger timestamps
tasks.description:
Two related layout issues in the reconciliation panel:

1. The grid-cols-2 layout in recon.html is hardcoded and doesn't stack on mobile. At 480px the left column (Event Buffer, Burst State, Watermarks, Verdict) gets crushed to ~200px and the Recent Runs table overflows horizontally.

2. The Trigger column in the Recent Runs table displays raw trigger_reason strings like "max_staleness:2026-03-19T06:59:04.491867+00:00" which are very long ISO timestamps that cause horizontal overflow even on desktop.

Fix the grid to be responsive (grid-cols-1 lg:grid-cols-2). Add a Jinja2 filter or inline logic to format trigger_reason strings — extract the trigger type and convert the timestamp to relative time, e.g. "max_staleness (1h ago)" or just "staleness" with the timestamp as a tooltip.
tasks.details:
Files to modify:
- dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/partials/recon.html line 1 — change grid-cols-2 to grid-cols-1 lg:grid-cols-2
- dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/partials/recon.html trigger column — add truncation/formatting for trigger_reason values
- dashboard/src/dashboard/app.py — add a trigger_format Jinja2 filter if needed

The trigger_reason format is "type:value" where type is one of: quiescent, buffer_size, max_staleness, manual. For max_staleness, the value is an ISO timestamp. For quiescent and buffer_size, the value is a number. Format as:
- "quiescent:6" → "quiescent (6)"
- "buffer_size:10" → "buffer (10)"
- "max_staleness:2026-03-19T..." → "staleness (1h ago)"
- "manual" → "manual"

Also add overflow-x-auto to the Recent Runs table container as a fallback.
tasks.testStrategy: Test the trigger_format filter with all four trigger types. Verify the recon partial HTML contains responsive grid classes. Visual verification at 480px and 768px widths.

tasks.title: Add section headings, loading skeletons, and fix timeago edge case
tasks.description:
Three minor UX issues grouped together:

1. No section headings for Memory and Reconciliation panels. Only "Orchestrators" has a visible heading. The page structure is unclear at a glance. Add "Memory" and "Reconciliation" headings to match the Orchestrators pattern.

2. When partials are loading, the user sees bare gray "Loading..." text with no visual indicator. Replace with a pulsing skeleton placeholder (Tailwind animate-pulse with gray placeholder boxes).

3. The timeago filter returns "0m ago" when total_minutes is 0 (event happened <60 seconds ago). Should return "just now" or "<1m ago" instead.
tasks.details:
Files to modify: - dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/index.html — add section headings above each hx-get div, replace "Loading..." with skeleton HTML - dashboard/src/dashboard/app.py timeago filter — handle the 0-minute case For skeletons, use something like:
For timeago: if total_minutes == 0, return "just now".
tasks.testStrategy: Test timeago filter with a timestamp from 30 seconds ago, verify it returns "just now". Test index.html contains section headings and animate-pulse classes.

tasks.title: Add HTMX error and timeout fallback for partial endpoints
tasks.description:
If a partial endpoint hangs or returns a server error, the HTMX section stays at "Loading..." permanently with no indication of failure. HTMX doesn't surface errors by default.

Add error handling so that failed partial loads show a visible error state rather than silent failure. Use HTMX's hx-on::after-request event or the htmx:responseError event to detect failures and show an error message in the target element.
tasks.testStrategy: Test that the error handler script is present in the base template. Integration test: mock a partial endpoint to return 500 and verify the HTMX error handling is wired up (check for the event listener in rendered HTML).

tasks.title: Fix worktree artifact lookup key mismatch with task IDs
tasks.description:
In tasks.html line 16, the template does orch.worktrees.get(tid, {}) where tid is the task ID as a string. But discover_orchestrators() in orchestrator.py keys worktrees by subdir.name (the directory name under .worktrees/). These only match if the worktree directory is named exactly as the task ID string.

The orchestrator names worktrees as "task-{id}" (e.g. "task-7"), not just "{id}". So the lookup always fails and every task shows dashes for Phase, Iterations, Reviews, and Modules columns.

Fix the key to match the orchestrator's naming convention, or normalize the worktree keys during discovery.
tasks.details:
Files to modify:
- dashboard/src/dashboard/data/orchestrator.py discover_orchestrators() — when building the worktrees dict, either:
  (a) Key by the task ID extracted from the directory name (strip "task-" prefix), or
  (b) Key by the raw directory name and update the template to use the correct key format

Option (a) is better — extract the task ID from worktree directory names like "task-7" → "7" and key the dict by "7".

Also check the actual worktree directory naming convention by looking at git_ops.py or the orchestrator's worktree creation code.
tasks.testStrategy: Test discover_orchestrators with a temp worktrees dir containing a "task-7" subdirectory. Verify the returned worktrees dict is keyed by "7" (the task ID), not "task-7".

tasks.title: Pin CDN dependencies with SRI hashes and add favicon
tasks.description:
Three issues with external dependencies and assets:

1. Alpine.js is loaded from alpinejs@3.x.x which auto-resolves to any 3.x version — could break without warning on a minor release.

2. HTMX is pinned to 2.0.4 but loaded from unpkg.com with no Subresource Integrity (SRI) hash, leaving it vulnerable to CDN compromise.

3. No favicon is served, causing a 404 console error on every page load.

Pin Alpine.js to a specific version (e.g. 3.14.8), add SRI integrity attributes to all CDN script tags, and add a minimal favicon (can be a simple SVG inline or a static file).
tasks.details:
Files to modify:
- dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/base.html — pin Alpine version, add integrity and crossorigin attributes to all CDN script tags
- dashboard/src/dashboard/static/ — add a favicon.ico or favicon.svg
- dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/base.html — add <link rel="icon"> in head

To generate SRI hashes: curl the CDN URL and pipe through `openssl dgst -sha384 -binary | openssl base64 -A`

For the favicon, a minimal SVG works:
<link rel="icon" href="data:image/svg+xml,<svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 100 100'><text y='.9em' font-size='90'>🏭</text></svg>">

Or create a simple static favicon.
tasks.testStrategy: Test that base.html contains integrity attributes on all CDN script tags. Test that GET /favicon.ico returns 200 (or that the inline SVG favicon link is present in the HTML head).

tasks.title: Deduplicate identical orchestrator cards when sharing task tree
tasks.description:
When multiple orchestrator processes are running against the same PRD, discover_orchestrators() loads the task tree once and attaches the identical data to all processes. The dashboard then shows two (or more) visually identical cards with the same task list, progress bar, and stats — confusing for users who expect each card to represent distinct work.

The fix should either:
1. Group orchestrators by PRD and show a single card with a note about multiple PIDs, or
2. Scope each orchestrator's task view to its actual working set (if the orchestrator's task assignment is tracked somewhere), or
3. At minimum, add visual differentiation (show all PIDs in the card header, note that they share the same task tree).

Option 3 is the simplest and most honest — show all PIDs sharing the same task tree in a single card.
tasks.details:
Files to modify:
- dashboard/src/dashboard/data/orchestrator.py discover_orchestrators() — group processes by PRD path, merge PIDs into a single entry
- dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/partials/orchestrators.html — show multiple PIDs in the header when grouped

In discover_orchestrators: after getting processes, group by proc['prd']. For each unique PRD, create one orchestrator entry with a 'pids' list instead of a single 'pid'. Attach the shared task tree and worktrees once.

In the template: show "PIDs 2109809, 2109812" instead of separate cards.
tasks.testStrategy: Test discover_orchestrators with mock ps output containing two processes with the same PRD. Verify the result contains one entry with both PIDs. Test the template renders multiple PIDs correctly.

tasks.title: Fix deprecated TemplateResponse API in recon route
tasks.description:
The recon partial route (app.py:102-112) uses the old Starlette TemplateResponse(name, {request: ...}) signature while the other three routes already use the new TemplateResponse(request, name, context=...) API. This produces 18 deprecation warnings in the test suite and will break on the next Starlette major version.

The fix is a one-line change: rewrite the call to match the pattern used by the memory and orchestrators routes.
tasks.testStrategy: Run pytest with -W error::DeprecationWarning to verify no deprecation warnings remain.

tasks.title: Taskmaster card hardcoded to 'Connected' — ignores actual status
tasks.description:
The Taskmaster card in memory.html (line 32-35) always renders a green dot and 'Connected' text regardless of the actual taskmaster connection status. The MCP get_status response includes a taskmaster.connected boolean, but the template doesn't reference it.

This means when Taskmaster is down, the dashboard still shows it as connected — directly misleading for the monitoring use case.
tasks.details:
File: dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/partials/memory.html lines 30-36 Current (hardcoded): ```html
...
Connected
``` Should conditionally check status.taskmaster.connected like the Graphiti and Mem0 cards do: ```html
...
{% if status.taskmaster.connected|default(false) %}Connected{% else %}Disconnected{% endif %}
``` Also add a test in test_memory_partial.py that verifies the card reflects actual taskmaster status.
tasks.testStrategy: Add test cases for taskmaster connected=true and connected=false. Verify the HTML contains the correct dot color and text for each state.

tasks.title: Narrow broad exception handlers and add logging for silent failures
tasks.description:
Three data modules silently swallow errors, making production debugging difficult:

1. orchestrator.py:35 catches bare `except Exception` on subprocess.run — this also catches KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit. Should be `except (OSError, subprocess.TimeoutExpired)`.

2. memory.py:52-53 and 65-66 silently return empty dicts on JSON parse failures with no logging. Malformed MCP responses vanish without a trace.

3. reconciliation.py has 5 identical try/except blocks catching FileNotFoundError and sqlite3.OperationalError with no logging.

All three should log at WARNING level when catching errors, and the orchestrator exception should be narrowed to specific types.
tasks.details:
Files to modify:
- dashboard/src/dashboard/data/orchestrator.py:35 — change `except Exception:` to `except (OSError, subprocess.TimeoutExpired):`
- dashboard/src/dashboard/data/memory.py:52,65 — add `logger.warning('MCP response parse error', exc_info=True)` before returning {}
- dashboard/src/dashboard/data/reconciliation.py — add logger and log at debug level in each except block

All three modules already import logging or can trivially add it. The key is to not change behavior (still return defaults) but make failures visible in logs.
tasks.testStrategy: Test that narrowed orchestrator exception still handles OSError. Test that a malformed MCP response triggers a log warning (use caplog fixture).

tasks.title: Memory cards don't wrap on narrow viewports — horizontal overflow
tasks.description:
The memory health cards use `flex gap-4` without `flex-wrap`, so the four cards (Graphiti, Mem0, Taskmaster, Write Queue) are always side-by-side. On viewports narrower than ~900px, the cards get crushed below readable widths. The PRD lists mobile as a non-goal, but the cards should at least wrap gracefully on smaller desktop/laptop windows.

Fix: add `flex-wrap` to the flex container and set `min-w-[200px]` on each card so they wrap to 2x2 or 1-column naturally.
tasks.details:
File: dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/partials/memory.html line 8

Current: `<div class="flex gap-4">`
Fix: `<div class="flex flex-wrap gap-4">`

Also add `min-w-[200px]` to each card div to ensure they wrap before becoming unreadably narrow.

This is visible in the audit screenshots at tablet (768px) and mobile (480px) widths where the Write Queue card text gets truncated.
tasks.testStrategy: Visual verification at 768px and 480px widths. Test that the memory partial HTML contains flex-wrap class.

tasks.title: Add ARIA labels to status indicator dots and auto-updating sections
tasks.description:
Status indicator dots (colored circles showing connected/disconnected) throughout the dashboard are purely visual — they have no aria-label, role, or screen-reader text. This means accessibility tools see empty divs.

Also, the three auto-polling sections (memory, recon, orchestrators) lack aria-live attributes, so screen readers won't announce updates.

Fix: add role='status' and aria-label to indicator dots, and aria-live='polite' to each polling section.
tasks.details:
Files to modify: - dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/partials/memory.html — add aria-label to each status dot div (e.g., aria-label='Graphiti connected' or 'Graphiti disconnected') - dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/partials/recon.html — add aria-label to status badges - dashboard/src/dashboard/templates/index.html — add aria-live='polite' to each hx-get section Example for memory.html: ```html
``` Use Jinja2 conditionals to set the label dynamically: ```html aria-label="Graphiti {% if status.graphiti.connected|default(false) %}connected{% else %}disconnected{% endif %}" ```
tasks.testStrategy: Test that rendered HTML contains aria-label attributes on status dots and aria-live on polling sections.

tasks.title: Extract duplicated badge color logic into Jinja2 macros
tasks.description:
Status badge HTML and color-mapping logic is duplicated 15+ times across four partial templates. Each template independently maps status strings to Tailwind color classes (e.g., 'done' → bg-green-600, 'blocked' → bg-red-600). Changes require updating every template, and inconsistencies are easy to introduce.

Extract a Jinja2 macro file with reusable badge components: status_badge(status), severity_badge(severity), and indicator_dot(connected).
tasks.testStrategy: Verify all partial templates render the same badge colors as before. Run existing test suite to ensure no visual regression.

tasks.title: Bug: Harness starts duplicate workflow for each task
tasks.description:
Every task's workflow is started twice by the harness — visible as duplicate "Starting workflow" and "acquired locks" log lines. The second workflow races the first through the pipeline. When the first completes and cleans the worktree, the second crashes trying to write review files (e.g. `reviewer_test_analyst.json` ENOENT), producing false blocking escalations that require manual resolution.

Observed on every task during the dashboard PRD run (tasks 33, 35, 38, 39, 40, 41). The real workflow completes and merges correctly, but the duplicate causes noise and blocks the orchestrator's final report (exit code 1).
tasks.details:
Root cause is likely in the harness's main loop where `scheduler.acquire_next()` is called and `asyncio.Task` objects are created. The acquire_next → workflow creation path probably runs twice before the task status transitions to in-progress.

Files to investigate:
- orchestrator/src/orchestrator/harness.py — the `while True` loop around `scheduler.acquire_next()` (lines ~145-170)
- orchestrator/src/orchestrator/scheduler.py — `acquire_next()` may return the same task twice if the status update hasn't propagated

Fix should ensure a task is only dispatched once, either by:
1. Tracking dispatched task IDs in a set and skipping duplicates
2. Atomically transitioning task status to in-progress before yielding from acquire_next
3. Using the module lock table as a guard (if task already holds locks, skip)

Test: Run orchestrator with 2+ tasks and verify each task appears exactly once in "Starting workflow" log lines.

queries: resolution for: Bug: Harness starts duplicate workflow for each task

tasks.title: Bug: FusedMemoryConfig defaults assume orchestrator/ cwd
tasks.description:
The `FusedMemoryConfig` class in `orchestrator/src/orchestrator/config.py` has defaults that only work if cwd is the orchestrator subdirectory:
- `url` defaults to `http://localhost:8000` (should match the project's port scheme)
- `config_path` defaults to `../fused-memory/config/config.yaml`
- `server_command` includes `--project ../fused-memory`

When running from the repo root without `--config` (common case), these resolve incorrectly. In a multi-project setup, the wrong port can connect to another project's fused-memory server — during the dashboard run, the orchestrator connected to reify's server on port 8000 and saw 0 pending tasks.
tasks.details:
Files to modify:
- orchestrator/src/orchestrator/config.py — FusedMemoryConfig class (line ~126)

Options:
1. Change defaults to be repo-root-relative: `url="http://localhost:8002"`, `config_path="fused-memory/config/config.yaml"`, `server_command` with `--project fused-memory`
2. Remove defaults entirely and require explicit config (fail fast with a clear error)
3. Derive defaults from project_root at config load time

Option 1 is simplest but hardcodes the dark-factory port. Option 3 is most robust — compute paths relative to project_root in a model_validator.

Also consider: the CLI's `settings_customise_sources` defaults to loading `config.yaml` from cwd. If no `--config` is passed and no `config.yaml` exists in cwd, it silently uses all defaults. Should it search upward or require explicit config?

Test: Run `orchestrator run --prd <prd>` from repo root without `--config` and verify it either uses correct defaults or fails with a helpful error.

queries: resolution for: Bug: FusedMemoryConfig defaults assume orchestrator/ cwd

tasks.title: Bug: update_task metadata parameter rejects dict/object values
tasks.description:
The fused-memory MCP tool `update_task` declares its `metadata` parameter as `string | null` in the Pydantic schema. Callers (including Claude Code's MCP tool invocation) naturally pass structured objects like `{"modules": ["a", "b"]}`, which get rejected with a validation error: "Input should be a valid string".

This forces workarounds like editing tasks.json directly or JSON-encoding the metadata before passing it, which is error-prone and defeats the purpose of the MCP tool abstraction.
tasks.details:
Files to modify:
- fused-memory/src/fused_memory/server/tools.py — the update_task tool's parameter definition for `metadata`

The parameter should accept `dict | str | null` — if a dict is passed, use it directly; if a string is passed, JSON-parse it first. This matches how callers naturally want to use it.

Check if other task tools (add_task, add_subtask) have the same issue with metadata parameters.

Test: Call update_task via MCP with metadata as both a dict and a JSON string. Verify both work and the task's metadata is updated correctly.

tasks.title: Scope verification to task modules instead of full repo
tasks.description: The verify step (orchestrator/src/orchestrator/verify.py) runs ruff check, pyright, and pytest across the entire repo. Pre-existing lint/type errors in modules outside a task's scope cause verification to fail even when the task's own code is clean. This is the single biggest source of wasted budget in orchestrator runs.
tasks.details:
## What happened

During the 2026-03-19 orchestrator run (7 tasks against dashboard PRD), tasks 42, 43, and 44 all had correct implementations with passing module-scoped tests. However, full-repo verification failed every attempt due to pre-existing issues:

- `shared/src/shared/usage_gate.py`: B007 (unused loop var), SIM105 (contextlib.suppress)
- `orchestrator/tests/test_verify.py`: I001 (import ordering)
- `orchestrator/tests/test_workflow_e2e.py`: 276 pyright errors (fake test classes not implementing Protocol ABCs)

Each task burned through 5 verify attempts × 2-4 debugger invocations, spending $8-15 per task on debug cycles that could never converge because the failures were outside the task's module scope.

## What was expected

Verification should only check files within the task's declared module scope. A task touching `fused-memory/src/fused_memory/reconciliation/` should not fail because `orchestrator/tests/test_workflow_e2e.py` has type errors.

## What I did as a workaround

1. Resolved escalations telling agents to proceed past out-of-scope failures
2. Manually verified task code was correct (`ruff check` and `pytest` on task-specific files)
3. Manually merged task branches after the orchestrator blocked them
4. Fixed the pre-existing lint issues on main (`usage_gate.py`, `test_verify.py`)

## Proposed fix

In `verify.py`, scope verification commands to the task's module paths:
- `ruff check <module_paths>` instead of `ruff check`
- `pyright <module_paths>` instead of `pyright`
- `pytest <test_paths>` instead of `pytest`

The task's modules are available in `.task/plan.json` (the `modules` and `files` fields) and in the task metadata. The verify step already receives the worktree path — it should also receive the module list.

Consider also a fallback: if module-scoped verification passes but full-repo fails, log a warning but don't block the task.

## Memory hints

- Search: "verify step scope modules" "verification pre-existing failures"
- Entities: verify.py, run_verification, ModuleConfig

tasks.title: Fix worktree venv isolation — imports resolve from main tree, not worktree
tasks.description: The orchestrator's worktrees share the main tree's .venv. Python imports resolve from the installed (main tree) package, not the worktree's modified source files. Tests in a worktree that exercise new code (new methods, changed signatures) fail because the import loads the old main-tree version.
tasks.details:
## What happened

During the 2026-03-19 run, tasks 42 and 44 both had this problem:

- Task 42 added `ModuleLockTable.is_held()` and `Scheduler._dispatched` to `orchestrator/src/orchestrator/scheduler.py` in its worktree. Tests in `.worktrees/42/orchestrator/tests/test_scheduler.py` imported `from orchestrator.scheduler import ModuleLockTable` — but this resolved to the main tree's installed package (via `.pth` file in the shared `.venv`), which didn't have `is_held()`. Result: `AttributeError: 'ModuleLockTable' object has no attribute 'is_held'`.

- Task 44 modified `fused-memory/src/fused_memory/reconciliation/stages/base.py` to accept `dict | str | None` for metadata. The test mock expected the new signature, but the import resolved to main's version with the old `str | None` signature. Result: `AssertionError` on mock call verification.

Both tasks passed all tests after merge to main (when the installed package matched the source).

Task 43's debugger also identified this: "The shared `.venv` resolves `orchestrator` from the **main tree** (via `.pth` file), not the worktree."

## What was expected

Tests in a worktree should import the worktree's source code, not the main tree's installed package.

## What I did as a workaround

Manually verified task code by reading diffs, then merged to main and confirmed tests passed post-merge.

## Proposed fix options

1. **Per-worktree `sys.path` injection**: Before running pytest in a worktree, prepend the worktree's `src/` directories to `PYTHONPATH`. This is the lightest fix — add it to `verify.py`'s subprocess environment.

2. **Per-worktree venv**: `uv sync` in each worktree. Correct but expensive (adds ~10-30s per task setup).

3. **Editable install override**: Run `uv pip install -e <worktree>/orchestrator --no-deps` into the shared venv before verification. Risky — concurrent tasks would clobber each other's installs.

4. **conftest.py path manipulation**: Add a `conftest.py` to worktree roots that does `sys.path.insert(0, ...)`. The orchestrator could generate this during `TaskArtifacts.init()`.

Option 1 is recommended — it's simple, isolated, and doesn't affect other concurrent tasks.

## Memory hints

- Search: "worktree venv import isolation" "sys.path worktree"
- Entities: verify.py, TaskArtifacts, git_ops.create_worktree

tasks.title: Prevent duplicate workflow from overriding completed task status
tasks.description: When the duplicate workflow bug (task 42) fires, the real workflow completes and sets the task to DONE, but the duplicate crashes later and sets the same task to BLOCKED — overriding DONE. The last status write wins, leaving correctly-completed tasks marked as blocked.
tasks.details:
## What happened

During the 2026-03-19 run, tasks 40 and 41 both completed successfully via the first (real) workflow:
- Task 40: DONE at 20:24:34, cost=$0.90, merged to main
- Task 41: DONE at 20:28:33, cost=$0.85, merged to main

The duplicate workflows for each task ran a second architect, which failed because the worktree had already been cleaned up by the successful merge:
- Task 40: duplicate architect at 20:24:55, error "'40'", set to BLOCKED
- Task 41: duplicate architect at 20:28:50, error "'41'", set to BLOCKED

The final task tree showed both tasks as BLOCKED despite being fully merged to main. I had to manually `set_task_status(id="40,41", status="done")`.

## What was expected

Once a task reaches DONE status, no subsequent status transition to BLOCKED should be allowed. The status machine should enforce: DONE is a terminal state.

## What I did as a workaround

Manually set task statuses back to done after the orchestrator run.

## Proposed fix

1. **Status transition guard in workflow.py**: Before setting a task to BLOCKED, check current status. If it's already DONE, skip the transition and log a warning.

2. **Task status FSM**: Enforce a proper finite state machine where DONE is terminal. Valid transitions: pending→in-progress→done, pending→in-progress→blocked, blocked→pending. Invalid: done→anything.

3. **This is partially addressed by task 42's `_dispatched` guard**: The duplicate won't start in the first place. But defense in depth is warranted — other edge cases (crash recovery, manual reruns) could also produce late status overwrites.

## Note

Task 42 (duplicate dispatch prevention) addresses the root cause. This task addresses the symptom defensively. Both are needed — task 42 prevents the common case, this task prevents the edge cases.

## Memory hints

- Search: "task status transition DONE BLOCKED" "duplicate workflow status"
- Entities: workflow.py, set_task_status, Scheduler

tasks.title: Auto-dismiss stale escalations on orchestrator startup
tasks.description: Unresolved escalations from prior orchestrator runs persist in the queue and block tasks in subsequent runs. The orchestrator picks up stale escalations as if they were from the current run, causing tasks to wait for resolution of issues that are no longer relevant.
tasks.details:
## What happened

During the 2026-03-19 run, 13 pending escalations from prior sessions were in the queue:
- esc-16-1 through esc-16-4: Task 16 scope violations (task already done)
- esc-19-1: Task 19 review cycles exhausted (task already done)
- esc-20-1: Task 20 review cycles exhausted (task already done)
- esc-22-2: Task 22 merge failed (task already done)
- esc-27-1: Task 27 verification exhausted (task already done)
- esc-34-2, esc-39-1: Post-merge verification failures (tasks already done)
- esc-38-2: Prior-run duplicate workflow crash for task 38

Task 38's current-run workflow was blocked by esc-38-2 (from the prior run) — it hit "waiting for escalation resolution" even though the escalation was about a worktree that no longer existed.

I had to manually dismiss all 13 stale escalations via `resolve_issue()` before the current run could proceed.

## What was expected

When a new orchestrator run starts, it should either:
1. Auto-dismiss all pending escalations from prior runs, or
2. Scope escalation lookups by run ID so old escalations don't block new runs

## What I did as a workaround

Manually called `resolve_issue(terminate=true)` on all 13 stale escalations.

## Proposed fix

In `harness.py` startup (before the task loop), add an escalation cleanup step:

```python
# Dismiss stale escalations from prior runs
pending = await escalation_client.get_pending_escalations()
for esc in pending:
    if esc.task_id in done_tasks or esc.timestamp < run_start_time:
        await escalation_client.resolve_issue(
            esc.id, resolution="Auto-dismissed: stale from prior run", terminate=True
        )
```

Alternatively, add a `run_id` field to escalations and only match escalations from the current run.

## Memory hints

- Search: "escalation stale prior run" "escalation cleanup startup"
- Entities: escalation.queue, harness.py, resolve_issue

tasks.title: Protect plan.json from concurrent overwrites by duplicate workflows
tasks.description: When the duplicate workflow bug fires, a second architect writes a completely different plan.json over the first architect's plan while the implementer is actively executing against the original plan. The implementer then cannot update step statuses because step IDs no longer match.
tasks.details:
## What happened

During the 2026-03-19 run, task 42 had two workflows running concurrently (the duplicate dispatch bug):

1. **Real workflow**: Architect wrote a 4-step plan using `is_held()` approach. Implementer executed all 4 steps, committing: efdb567, 2a5905d, e53c830, e78028b. All 41 tests passing.

2. **Duplicate workflow**: Second architect ran ~20 seconds later and wrote an entirely different 8-step plan using a `_dispatched: set[str]` approach. This overwrote `.task/plan.json` in the same worktree.

When the real implementer tried to update plan.json step statuses after completing all work, the step IDs and descriptions didn't match. The implementer escalated (esc-42-3) with: "Plan mismatch: plan.json on disk was replaced with a different 8-step plan while executing the original 4-step plan."

## What was expected

plan.json should be immutable after creation, or at minimum protected from concurrent writes.

## What I did as a workaround

Resolved the escalation telling the implementer that the original 4-step plan was authoritative and to proceed to verify/merge.

## Proposed fix

1. **Lock file**: Architect writes a `.task/plan.lock` after creating plan.json. Subsequent architect invocations check for the lock and skip if present.

2. **Version header**: plan.json includes a `created_by` session ID and `created_at` timestamp. The implementer validates these match before updating. If they don't match, it escalates rather than silently failing.

3. **Root cause already addressed**: Task 42's `_dispatched` guard prevents duplicate workflows, so the second architect never runs. But defense in depth is warranted for edge cases (crash recovery re-invokes architect, manual rerun with stale worktree).

## Note

This is partially mitigated by task 42 (dispatch guard). This task adds defense in depth at the artifact layer.

## Memory hints

- Search: "plan.json overwrite concurrent architect" "duplicate workflow plan"
- Entities: TaskArtifacts, plan.json, architect agent

tasks.title: Bug: Scheduler dispatches tasks with in-progress dependencies (premature dispatch)
tasks.description: The orchestrator scheduler only checks whether dependencies are done or pending when deciding if a task is eligible for dispatch. Dependencies that are in-progress are treated as satisfied, causing dependent tasks to start before their prerequisites complete. Confirmed active on 2026-03-20 run — caused tasks 47 and 49 to start prematurely, wasting ~$10 in LLM cost.
tasks.details:
## Root cause

scheduler.py's `acquire_next()` (or equivalent dependency check) evaluates dependency status and only blocks on pending — it does not treat in-progress as a blocking state. A task should only be dispatched when ALL declared dependencies have status=done.

## Fix

In scheduler.py, update the dependency eligibility check so that a task is only considered ready when every dependency has status==done. If any dependency is in-progress or pending, the task must wait.

Pseudo-code:
```python
def _deps_satisfied(task) -> bool:
    for dep_id in task.dependencies:
        dep = get_task(dep_id)
        if dep.status != "done":   # was: dep.status not in ("done",)
            return False
    return True
```

## Impact

Without this fix, multiple tasks can start simultaneously when they should be sequential, causing:
- Duplicate/conflicting work
- Wasted LLM cost (~$10 observed on 2026-03-20)
- Race conditions in shared worktrees

## Memory hints

- Search: "scheduler dependency gating in-progress premature dispatch"
- Search: "acquire_next scheduler dependency check status"
- Entities: scheduler.py, acquire_next, Scheduler

tasks.title: Fix: Stage 3 integrity checker reads from stale Graphiti instance (ports 8000/8001)
tasks.description: Stage 3 reconciliation reports specific Graphiti edge UUIDs that do not exist in the canonical graph (port 8002 / systemd unit). Stage 3 is likely querying stale instances on ports 8000/8001. Until resolved, Stage 3 edge UUIDs will be phantom references that Stage 1 cannot act on, generating false findings every cycle.
tasks.details:
## Evidence
- Stage 1 (run 518e46bf) flagged this as HIGH priority (flag_id B, finding_source new_this_run).
- Stage 3 reported specific edge UUIDs (9e997aba-0357-447b-8134-5ece0e4d1eb4, 08a82582-f614-4d9b-9f38-0a7d8560c2e3, 71dec7cd-8c13-42e2-a4bc-37209d7e8668, 8f0a5980-2e36-49f4-9d5d-74fcba5a59a4) that Stage 1 confirmed do not exist in canonical graph at port 8002.
- Established project knowledge: "Stale MCP Instances on Ports 8000/8001 — Claude Code MCP instances on 8000/8001 run old code; systemd on 8002 is canonical." (memory: project_stale_mcp_ports.md)
- Stage 3 is read-only (confirmed in Graphiti — Stage 3 IntegrityCheck disallows all writes for MCP tool names).

## Root Cause
Stage 3 may be resolving edge UUIDs against a Graphiti endpoint configured to port 8000 or 8001 instead of 8002. Any edges that exist only in the stale instance (written before the systemd unit was established) will appear to Stage 3 but not to Stage 1 or interactive queries.

## Steps
1. Locate Stage 3 configuration: find the Stage 3 reconciliation script/config in `fused-memory/` — check for hardcoded port, environment variable, or MCP connection config.
2. Verify: confirm that Stage 3's Graphiti client connects to port 8002 only.
3. If misconfigured: update Stage 3 config/code to use the canonical port 8002.
4. Re-run Stage 3 and confirm that the previously-reported phantom edge UUIDs no longer appear.
5. Write a memory confirming the fix (category: decisions_and_rationale).

## Related
- The 4 phantom edge UUIDs (9e997aba, 08a82582, 71dec7cd, 8f0a5980) should NOT be treated as real edges — do not attempt deletion until Stage 3 is fixed and re-confirms their existence.

## Memory hints (execute before starting)

- Search: "stale MCP instances ports 8000 8001 canonical 8002"

tasks.title: Investigate and resolve accelerating DLQ dead letter accumulation
tasks.description: The dead-letter queue (DLQ) has reached 124 entries and is growing +18 per reconciliation run (up from +8/cycle previously). This is degrading the reliability of the reconciliation pipeline. Diagnose the current source of new dead letters, fix the root cause, and replay the accumulated dead letters to restore queue health.
tasks.details:
## Context
- Current DLQ count: 124 dead letters (as of recon run fbc232ba, 2026-03-23)
- Growth rate: +18 this run; prior runs showed +8/cycle (accelerating)
- Prior count at end of run 3d681930: 89; grew to 106 before this run, now 124

## Historical root cause
A FalkorDB vector dimension mismatch was the root cause identified on 2026-03-21. Previous fix attempts included an index rebuild + DLQ replay, but dead letters continue to accumulate.

## Steps
1. Inspect dead letters: `mcp__fused-memory__replay_dead_letters` (dry-run first) to see error patterns
2. Check FalkorDB vector index dimensions match current embedding model
3. If dimension mismatch persists, rebuild FalkorDB vector index (idempotent operation)
4. Identify any new sources of dead letters beyond dimension mismatch
5. Replay dead letters after root cause is fixed
6. Monitor for 2 runs to confirm growth stops

tasks.title: Fix FalkorDB vector index: re-index with 1536-dimension embeddings to resolve dead-letter queue
tasks.description: 2 dead-letter queue items remain unreplayable due to a vector dimension mismatch: the FalkorDB index expects 1536 dimensions but the queued writes contain 1024-dimension vectors from a prior embedding model. The dead-letter items cannot be replayed until the index is rebuilt for the current embedding model dimensions.
tasks.details:
## Problem
Queue stats: 2 dead, 0 pending. Dead items fail on replay with vector dimension mismatch (expected 1536, got 1024). Root cause: stale FalkorDB vector index created under a prior embedding model that used 1024-dim vectors. Current model (OpenAI text-embedding-3-small or similar) uses 1536 dims.

## Fix Steps
1. Identify the affected FalkorDB index (likely the Graphiti vector index for embeddings)
2. Drop the stale 1024-dim vector index from FalkorDB
3. Recreate the index with 1536-dim vectors (matching current embedding model)
4. Re-embed any existing nodes that were indexed under the old 1024-dim model
5. Call `replay_dead_letters(project_id="dark_factory")` to retry the 2 dead items
6. Verify queue stats show dead=0

## Context
- FalkorDB runs on port 6379
- Graphiti uses FalkorDB as its backend graph store
- Index creation is idempotent (dropping and recreating is safe)
- Config: fused-memory/config/config.yaml
